trajectory_summary: The call opens with a warm, informed prospect who has a real project in mind and re-engages twice after Corey's close-out with substantive questions about net metering and battery storage. Corey DQs on the workshop-not-built basis without ever pivoting to the existing home, then exits the call before Andy's actual interest is fully surfaced. The result is a soft callback with no locked time, a personal number text as the only follow-up mechanism, and two enthusiasm signals (battery storage, net metering curiosity) that were acknowledged but not converted into a booking attempt.
outcome_dispute: The v1 tag is 'unknown' — assumed not-booked based on transcript evidence. No appointment was set. Corey issued a soft callback ('once you have a roof design built out, give me a call directly') with no locked time and no qualification of the existing home. This is a missed booking on a warm, re-engaging lead, not a clean DQ. The outcome should be tagged as missed_opportunity / soft_callback rather than clean_DQ or not_qualified.

primary_coaching_focus.quote: So we are unable to help you right now without anything built or, like, something to work off of with, like, a roof design or anything.
primary_coaching_focus.why: Corey DQs on the workshop-not-built basis without ever asking about the existing home at the confirmed address. This is the single move that would have converted a soft callback into a qualified booking. The pivot is simple and low-risk — 'What about the home you're in right now?' — and Andy's subsequent re-engagement (net metering, battery storage) confirms he had the interest to support it. This is the coaching priority because it is a repeatable structural gap: any time a customer leads with a future build, Corey needs the habit of asking about the existing structure before issuing a DQ.

key_moments.quote: So we are unable to help you right now without anything built or, like, something to work off of with, like, a roof design or anything.
key_moments.stewart_read: Corey DQs on the workshop-not-built basis without ever asking about the existing home at 376 Seville Road. The address was confirmed at 01:46 — Corey had it in hand. The strategic read would be that Corey recognized the workshop lead was premature and was correctly managing scope. The reactive read is that Corey accepted Andy's framing entirely and never introduced the existing home as a qualifying candidate. The reactive read holds: there is no evidence Corey considered the existing home, and the DQ language ('without anything built') is framed entirely around the future workshop.

key_moments.quote: And we don't do ground mounts here just because they are super expensive and ultimately defeat the purpose of savings.
key_moments.stewart_read: Corey delivers an editorial economics judgment — ground mounts are 'super expensive and ultimately defeat the purpose of savings' — that belongs to the closer, not the setter. The schema's scope_creep_signals explicitly flag 'rep quoting prices or savings ranges' and 'rep justifying company practices that customer questions.' Corey is not answering a customer question here; he is volunteering a cost-based rationale for a product limitation. This is setter-scope content delivered as a final verdict.

key_moments.quote: I'm fairly certain they're now doing the selling of the power, but I'm not too certain on that.
key_moments.stewart_read: Andy asks a specific, technical question about Rappahannock Electric Cooperative's net metering policy. Corey gives a hedged, uncertain answer rather than routing to the specialist. This is a double miss: (1) Corey is engaging in closer-tier content (utility net metering rules) outside setter scope, and (2) the answer is uncertain, which erodes credibility. The correct move is the universal escape hatch — 'That's exactly the kind of question our specialist is built to answer; they'll know Rappahannock's current policy' — which simultaneously holds scope and creates a reason to book.

key_moments.quote: I would like us I would be interested in the system that has battery banks. Yep. And is that something you could you you could help help me with?
key_moments.stewart_read: Andy volunteers an explicit interest in battery storage — a Tesla Powerwall product Ion carries. This is a direct enthusiasm signal: Andy is not just curious about solar, he wants a specific product configuration. Corey confirms capability ('Of course. Yes.') and ends the call. The confirmation is correct but the follow-through is absent. This is the second re-entry point Andy handed Corey after the DQ, and it was not used to pivot back to qualification or booking.
